mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers Christianity including its history, major forms (Orthodox, Catholic, Protestant), church institutions, and doctrines, the primary subject of 827.
Examines the life, death, resurrection, and teachings of Jesus Christ, directly relevant to 827.A.1.a and 827.F.1.c on Christology.
Explores the apostle Paul's theology of faith, salvation, and resurrection, shedding light on the origins of the primitive church in 827.A.1.a.
Traces Augustine's thinking on grace, original sin, creation, and the Donatist controversy, directly relevant to 827.A.2.b on doctrinal disputes of the patristic era.
Engages with Aquinas's Summa Theologiae and his theology of creation and natural law, relevant to 827.C.1.b on Latin Christianity and 827.E.7 on Christian philosophy.
Presents Anselm's satisfaction theory of atonement, the ontological argument for God's existence, and his role in the investiture controversy, relevant to 827.C.1 and 827.E.7.
Traces Luther's break with Rome over indulgences and justification by faith, along with the German Reformation he ignited, directly relevant to 827.D.1.b.
Addresses the Bible as foundational Christian scripture and the history of its interpretation, directly relevant to 827.E.1 on biblical literature.
Surveys the Old Testament as part of Christian biblical literature, including the Pentateuch, prophetic books, and wisdom writings, relevant to 827.E.1.
Directly covers the New Testament including its canon, the Holy Spirit, and the Kingdom of God, relevant to 827.E.1.
Investigates non-canonical early Christian texts such as the Gospel of Thomas and the Nag Hammadi library, including their Gnostic dimensions, relevant to 827.E.1 on the Apocrypha.
Explores the theory of biblical interpretation from patristic allegory through modern historical-critical methods, directly relevant to 827.E.2 on exegesis and hermeneutics.
Surveys Christian theology broadly, from doctrines of creation and prayer to inter-faith dialogue, relevant to 827.E and 827.F.
Examines Christian doctrines concerning God, including the Trinity, creation ex nihilo, and Aquinas's five ways, relevant to 827.F.1.
Directly covers Christian art and iconography, the subject of 827.F.7.
Analyzes Christian ethical teaching on virtue, the morality of abortion, and economic justice, relevant to 827.F.4 and 827.G.3.c on Protestantism's influence on social thought.
Directly covers Roman Catholicism including sacraments, creation, and sacred traditions, the subject of 827.G.2.
Directly covers Eastern Orthodoxy including the East-West Schism, the subject of 827.B and 827.G.1.
Directly covers Protestantism including Lutheranism, Calvinism, Methodism, and the World Council of Churches, the subject of 827.D and 827.G.3.
Directly covers the Protestant Reformation including Lutheran, Calvinist, and Anabaptist movements, the subject of 827.C.2 and 827.D.1.
Details the Counter-Reformation response to Protestantism, including the Council of Trent's doctrinal decrees and Jesuit missionary activity, the subject of 827.C.2.c and 827.D.1.g.
Explores Calvin's covenant theology, predestination, and model of church governance, directly relevant to 827.G.3.d.ii on Reformed and Presbyterian churches.
Directly covers the Anglican Communion including the Church of England, the subject of 827.G.3.d.iii.
Examines the Puritan movement's Calvinist roots, emphasis on godly discipline, and the Protestant ethic, relevant to 827.D.2.d-e on English and American Protestantism.
Directly covers Methodism and the Wesley brothers, one of the Free churches listed in 827.G.3.d.iv.
Directly covers Pentecostalism including healing and holiness traditions, the subject of 827.G.3.e.ii.
Traces the evangelical movement from its revivalist origins to the modern megachurch phenomenon, relevant to 827.G.3.e.vii on evangelical groups.
Directly covers the Society of Friends (Quakers) including George Fox and worship practices, the subject of 827.G.3.e.iv.
Directly covers Mormonism, the subject of 827.H.3.
Assesses the Second Vatican Council's sweeping reforms to Catholic liturgy, theology, and ecumenical relations, relevant to 827.C.5.
Traces Marian devotion from the early church through Reformation-era debates and modern feminist theology, relevant to 827.F on beliefs and practices.
Traces the development of Christian monastic life from the Desert Fathers through Benedictine and mendicant orders, relevant to 827.F.6.c on monasticism as a form of Christian life.
Explores the Christian tradition of sainthood, canonization, and the cult of relics, relevant to 827.F.6.c on the saintly life.
Traces Christian martyrdom from the persecution of the early church under Rome through modern witness, relevant to 827.A.1.b on post-apostolic developments.
Examines angelology in Christian tradition, from the celestial hierarchies of Pseudo-Dionysius to popular devotion, relevant to 827.F.2 on doctrines concerning intermediary beings.
Traces the evolving Christian concept of Satan from the New Testament through medieval demonology, directly relevant to 827.F.2 on doctrines concerning Satan.
Investigates the philosophical and theological status of miracles in Christian belief, relevant to 827.F.1.c on Christology and claims about Jesus.
Explores Christian pilgrimage traditions from Jerusalem and Canterbury to Lourdes and Santiago de Compostela, relevant to 827.F.6 on practices common among Christians.
Examines Christianity's engagement with science, from the Galileo affair to modern debates over evolution, relevant to 827.A.2.c on the relation of Christian religion to culture.
Analyzes Christian fundamentalism and its commitment to biblical inerrancy, relevant to 827.G.3.e.vii on fundamentalist groups.
Illuminates the era when Christianity became the Roman state religion and contended with Manichaeism and other rivals, directly relevant to 827.A.2.
Explores Byzantine civilization and its deep entanglement with Eastern Christianity, from the construction of Hagia Sophia to iconoclasm, relevant to 827.B.1.
The Crusades were a major expression of medieval Christianity, relevant to 827.C.1.
Depicts the medieval world in which Christianity shaped every aspect of European culture and the papacy wielded enormous power, relevant to 827.C.1.
Traces Christian scholasticism from Boethius through William of Ockham, showing how philosophy served theology in the medieval university, relevant to 827.E.7 on Christian philosophy.
Renaissance art is largely Christian in subject matter, relevant to 827.F.7 on major eras and schools of Christian art.
The Enlightenment challenged Christianity with rationalism and Deism, directly relevant to 827.D.2.c.
The Holy Roman Empire's history is intertwined with the papacy and the Reformation, relevant to 827.C.1.a and 827.D.1.
Examines Stuart-era religious conflicts among Puritans, Anglicans, and Catholics that reshaped English Protestantism, relevant to 827.D.2.d.
Places Christianity in comparative perspective with Judaism and Islam, illuminating its shared Abrahamic roots and distinctive developments.
Surveys Christianity in the American context, from Methodist circuit riders to new religious movements, relevant to 827.D.2.e and 827.G.3.
Illuminates the African American Christian tradition and the Black church's role in spiritual life and social justice, directly relevant to 827.G.3.e.viii.
Traces the idea of religious toleration through the wars of religion and confessional schisms, relevant to 827.D.1.h on the development of religious liberty.
Dante's Divine Comedy is a supreme expression of medieval Christian theology and eschatology, relevant to 827.E and 827.F.1.d.
C. S. Lewis was a major Christian apologist, relevant to 827.E.5 on writings of theologians and church leaders.
Kierkegaard is a major Christian philosopher addressing faith and subjectivity, relevant to 827.E.7 on Christian philosophy.
Analyzes the secularization of historically Christian societies and the challenges posed to organized religion, relevant to 827.D.4 on modern Protestantism and the contemporary context.
Explores Christian spiritual and mystical traditions, from Meister Eckhart to contemplative prayer, relevant to 827.E.6 on Christian mysticism.
Probes the nature of Christian faith, the interplay of belief and reason, and how confessional identity is articulated, relevant to 827.E.4 on creeds and confessions of faith.
Engages with the existence and nature of God (F.1), the problem of evil (F.2), and the relationship between faith and reason, directly relevant to Christian doctrines and philosophy (E.7).
